_meta.produced_by: Instance 1 (Claude, Anthropic) — Phase 5 retroactive AI layer build
amendments: A-S43-1: methodology_D added to paper_type enum
A-S43-2: IR-8 Parsimony/Modeling Principle Selection added to inference rule inventory

paper_title: Meta-Kaizen: A General Theory and Algorithmic Framework for the Mathematical Formalization of Self-Evolving Continuous Improvement Across Arbitrary Governance Substrates
paper_type_note: Primary contribution is the axiomatic derivation of KVS functional form (Theorem 4.1). Paper also contains application demonstrations (Section 6) and pre-registered empirical path (Section 5.4). Classified derivation_B because the derivation is the novel claim; applications are illustrations, not the object of verification. The schema enum [law_A, derivation_B, application_C] does not include 'methodology'. See paper_type_flag below.
paper_type_flag: RESOLVED S43: derivation_B confirmed for MK-P1. CBT Amendment A-S43-1 adds methodology_D to schema enum for DRP-1 and future methodology papers.

phase_ready.retroactive_note: Paper prose (v8) predates Dual-Reader Standard adoption (S39). AI layer is additive — no prose changes made or required. Retroactive AI layer produces first live Channel 2 asset and resolves PH-DRP.2 (DRP-1 Section 7.4 live instance).
phase_ready.c3_evaluability: SATISFIED — all Type F predicates are finite, computable, and free of natural language interpretation steps in their EVALUATION clauses
phase_ready.c5_gate: SATISFIED — this document is the Phase 5 gate output
phase_ready.c6_falsification_test: SATISFIED — sample falsification observations provided for each Type F claim

falsification_syntax: I-2 5-part (FALSIFIED IF / WHERE / EVALUATION / BOUNDARY / CONTEXT)
inference_rule_inventory: IR-1 through IR-7 — Fracttalix Phase 1-2 Merged, S42, Section HR-1.1.C resolution

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: Axiom 1 — Weak Order
claim_registry.statement: For any two improvement candidates a and b, either a is at least as good as b or b is at least as good as a, and this ordering is transitive.
claim_registry.axiomatic_basis: Minimum condition for any measurement to be meaningful. Standard conjoint measurement axiom (Luce & Tukey, 1964).
claim_registry.note: Type A: taken as given. Reject only by rejecting the measurement framework itself.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: Axiom 2 — Preferential Independence
claim_registry.statement: The preference ordering over candidates that differ only in component N is independent of the values of I′, C′, and T. Holds symmetrically for all four components.
claim_registry.axiomatic_basis: Luce & Tukey (1964) standard conjoint measurement starting point.
claim_registry.modeling_assumption_note: Paper explicitly flags this as a modeling assumption, not an empirical regularity. Violations require differential weights or non-additive-in-logs model. Identified as target for empirical testing in Section 5.4 and Section 7.6.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: Axiom 3 — Solvability
claim_registry.statement: For any three components held fixed, there exists a value of the fourth component that achieves any target quality score in [0,1]. Ensures scale is continuous and complete.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: Axiom 4 — Archimedean Condition
claim_registry.statement: No finite sequence of equal-interval steps in any component is unbounded. No component can be infinitely more important than another.
claim_registry.axiomatic_basis: Standard conjoint measurement axiom (Luce & Tukey, 1964; Krantz et al., 1971).

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: Axiom 5 — Essentialness with Veto Power
claim_registry.statement: A zero value on any single component renders the candidate inadoptable: f(0,I′,C′,T) = f(N,0,C′,T) = f(N,I′,0,T) = f(N,I′,C′,0) = 0 for all component values.
claim_registry.axiomatic_basis: Novel axiom introduced in this paper. Defense: N=0 is reimplementation; I′=0 produces no improvement; C′=0 costs overwhelm benefit; T=0 relevance window passed.
claim_registry.note: This is the axiom that eliminates the additive form and necessitates the multiplicative form. Rejecting Axiom 5 is explicitly permitted — the library implements both KVS and ADD variants.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:A]
claim_registry.name: Axiom 6 — Marginal Symmetry
claim_registry.statement: The marginal rate of substitution between any two components is equal at all points where their values are equal. Formally: ∂f/∂N = ∂f/∂I′ whenever N = I′; and symmetrically for all pairs.
claim_registry.axiomatic_basis: Justifies equal weights in the absence of domain-specific calibration data. Relaxing this axiom requires replacing equal exponents with estimated per-component exponents.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Governance Substrate
claim_registry.statement: A substrate S is a tuple S = (D, P, Ω, M) where D = document set, P = principal set, Ω = outcome space, M = metric function M: D × time → Ω. An improvement candidate for S is a proposed revision δ to D characterized by Novelty, Impact, Complexity, and Timeliness.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: KVS Formula
claim_registry.statement: KVS_j = N_j × I′_j × C′_j × T_j, where N_j, I′_j, C′_j, T_j ∈ [0,1] are defined in D-MK1.3 through D-MK1.6.
claim_registry.derivation_note: This form is derived, not stipulated. Derivation traced in F-MK1.3.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Novelty Factor
claim_registry.statement: N_j = 1 − max_{τ ∈ {t−1,t−2,t−3,t−4}} Jaccard(K_j, K_τ), where Jaccard(A,B) = |A∩B|/|A∪B|. Maximum (not mean) Jaccard similarity used. Operational target: E[N] ≥ 0.70 over rolling 4-scan window.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Normalized Impact
claim_registry.statement: I′_j = min(1, μ_j / I_max), where μ_j is the administrator's point estimate of annualized improvement and I_max is the pre-committed substrate-specific maximum. Recommended I_max values: IPS = 3.0 pp/yr; clinical = 5.0 pp; DORA = 50%; lean = 10% waste reduction.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Normalized Inverse Complexity
claim_registry.statement: C′_j = (2.0 − C_j) / 1.0, where C_j ∈ {1.0, 1.2, 1.4, 1.6, 1.8, 2.0}. Linear rescaling maps [1.0, 2.0] bijectively onto [1.0, 0.0], fully spanning [0,1] and reaching zero at maximum complexity. Corrects prior 1/C formulation which compressed range to [1.0, 0.5].

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:D]
claim_registry.name: Timeliness Score
claim_registry.statement: T_j = max(0, 1 − h_j / H_max), where h_j = months to relevance and H_max = 60 (default). H_max revisable at annual review with principal approval.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Property 4.1 — Boundedness
claim_registry.statement: KVS_j ∈ [0,1] for all improvement candidates j.

derivation_trace.operation: IR-4: Definition Expansion — each component is bounded in [0,1] by its definition
IR-5: Algebraic Manipulation — product of four numbers each in [0,1] is in [0,1]

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: O = {candidate j with N_j = 1.1} → KVS_j > 1 → FALSIFIED → PASS (D-MK1.3 prevents N_j > 1)

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS improvement candidate j SUCH THAT (KVS_j < 0) OR (KVS_j > 1)

WHERE.j: any improvement candidate with N_j, I′_j, C′_j, T_j ∈ [0,1] per D-MK1.3 through D-MK1.6

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Compute N_j × I′_j × C′_j × T_j for candidate j; check result lies in [0,1]; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: KVS_j = 0 or KVS_j = 1 → NOT FALSIFIED (boundary values are permitted)
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: This property follows immediately from D-MK1.2: the product of four numbers each in [0,1] is in [0,1]. It is a Property rather than a Theorem because it requires no non-trivial inference. Stated for architectural significance: ensures cross-substrate comparability without renormalization.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Property 4.2 — Monotonicity
claim_registry.statement: KVS_j is non-decreasing in each of N_j, I′_j, C′_j, T_j, holding others fixed. For any factor f_k: ∂KVS_j/∂f_k = ∏_{i≠k} f_i ≥ 0.

derivation_trace.operation: IR-5: Algebraic Manipulation — partial derivative ∂KVS_j/∂f_k = ∏_{i≠k} f_i
derivation_trace.output: ∂KVS_j/∂f_k expressed as product of remaining components

derivation_trace.operation: IR-4: Definition Expansion — all f_i ≥ 0 by construction

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: O = {j with N=0.5, j′ with N=0.6, all others equal at 0.8} → KVS_j = 0.5×0.512 = 0.256; KVS_j′ = 0.6×0.512 = 0.307 → KVS_j′ > KVS_j → NOT FALSIFIED → PASS

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS improvement candidates j, j′ SUCH THAT (j′ dominates j on exactly one component, all others equal) AND (KVS_j′ < KVS_j)

WHERE.j_dominates_j_prime_on_k: f_k(j′) > f_k(j) AND f_i(j′) = f_i(j) for all i ≠ k
WHERE.k: any single component from {N, I′, C′, T}

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: For any pair (j, j′) differing on exactly one component, compute both KVS scores; check KVS_j′ ≥ KVS_j; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: KVS_j′ = KVS_j when the improved component starts at or contributes zero product — NOT FALSIFIED (non-decreasing, not strictly increasing)
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: Significance is normative: correct incentive structure. An improvement candidate that scores higher on any dimension should never score lower overall.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Theorem 4.1 — KVS Functional Form (three-part)
claim_registry.statement: Under Axioms 1–6 and the Scale Normalization Condition, the canonical functional form representing improvement quality is KVS_j = N_j × I′_j × C′_j × T_j. (a) Multiplicative structure follows necessarily from Axiom 5. (b) Equal exponents follow necessarily from Axiom 6. (c) Common exponent α = 1 is the canonical modeling choice under Scale Normalization — not a uniquely forced mathematical result.
claim_registry.claim_structure_note: Three-part claim with distinct epistemic status. Parts (a) and (b) are necessary derivations — falsified if the logical chain contains an invalid step. Part (c) is a canonical selection — falsified if a stronger parsimony argument for α ≠ 1 is produced while satisfying all six axioms and the proportional normalization condition.

derivation_trace.operation: IR-1: Modus Ponens — Luce-Tukey (1964) theorem establishes: Axioms 1–4 → additive representation F = Σφ_i(x_i)
derivation_trace.output: F = φ_N(N) + φ_I(I′) + φ_C(C′) + φ_T(T), unique up to positive affine transformations

derivation_trace.operation: IR-3: Substitution of Equals — substitute N=0 into additive form
derivation_trace.output: φ_N(0) + φ_I(I′) + φ_C(C′) + φ_T(T) = 0 for all I′, C′, T

derivation_trace.operation: IR-5: Algebraic Manipulation — rearrange to isolate φ_I + φ_C + φ_T
derivation_trace.output: φ_I(I′) + φ_C(C′) + φ_T(T) = −φ_N(0) for all I′, C′, T

derivation_trace.operation: IR-1: Modus Ponens — if LHS varies with arguments but RHS is constant, contradiction
derivation_trace.output: Contradiction: functions cannot simultaneously vary and equal a constant

derivation_trace.operation: IR-1: Modus Ponens — contradiction eliminates additive form
IR-2: Universal Instantiation — multiplicative form N^α × I′^β × C′^γ × T^δ satisfies all five axioms
IR-5: Algebraic Manipulation — compute ∂f/∂N and ∂f/∂I′ under multiplicative form
IR-1: Modus Ponens — apply Axiom 6: set ∂f/∂N = ∂f/∂I′ at N=I′
IR-5: Algebraic Manipulation — at N=I′, (I′/N)^(β−α) = 1^(β−α) = 1 → α/β = 1 → α = β
IR-2: Universal Instantiation — same argument applied symmetrically to all pairs of components
derivation_trace.output: α = β = γ = δ; form reduces to (N×I′×C′×T)^α for some α > 0

derivation_trace.operation: IR-4: Definition Expansion — components are normalized to [0,1] with proportional interpretation
derivation_trace.output: Each component value x represents x-proportion of its maximum

derivation_trace.operation: IR-8: Parsimony / Modeling Principle Selection — Scale Normalization Condition selects φ_i(x_i) = x_i as canonical identity scale for proportionally-interpreted components (Krantz et al., 1971, Ch. 9, §9.2.1). Valid: family of consistent options established (any α > 0); parsimony principle named and cited; selection documented as canonical choice not necessary derivation.
derivation_trace.output: α = 1 selected as canonical choice; any α > 0 axiom-consistent

derivation_trace.operation: IR-3: Substitution of Equals — substitute α = 1 into (N×I′×C′×T)^α

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: O = {Step S2.3: additive form satisfies Axiom 5 if φ_N(0)=0 and φ_i(0)=0 for all i} → tests whether the zero-point assignment rescues the additive form. Assessment: if all φ_i(0)=0, then Σφ_i = 0 only when all components are at their minimal value, not for all values of I′, C′, T at N=0 — Stage 2 proof survives → NOT FALSIFIED → PASS

WHERE.n_invalid_steps_stage2: integer · count of derivation steps in Stage 2 (Section 4.1) that fail validity: step is valid iff output follows from inputs by a named rule from IR-1 through IR-7
WHERE.additive_form_satisfies_A5: boolean · TRUE if f(0,I′,C′,T) = 0 is consistent with additive form F = φ_N(N) + φ_I(I′) + φ_C(C′) + φ_T(T) without requiring φ_I, φ_C, φ_T to be constants

part_a_multiplicative.EVALUATION: Trace Stage 2 derivation step-by-step; assign each step a named rule from IR-1 through IR-7; flag any step without a valid rule; check whether additive form can satisfy Axiom 5; finite
part_a_multiplicative.BOUNDARY: A step classified under a new inference rule not in IR-1 through IR-7 is an open gap (PLACEHOLDER) pending CBT amendment; it is not automatically a falsification
part_a_multiplicative.CONTEXT: Stage 2 is the core proof: setting N=0 in additive form requires φ_I(I′)+φ_C(C′)+φ_T(T) = −φ_N(0) for all I′,C′,T — impossible unless all three are constants, contradicting that they vary.

WHERE.n_invalid_steps_stage3: integer · count of invalid derivation steps in Stage 3 (Section 4.1)
WHERE.stage3_conclusion: string · algebraic conclusion of Stage 3 under A-MK1.6 applied to multiplicative form

part_b_equal_exponents.EVALUATION: Trace Stage 3 derivation; verify each algebraic step under IR-5; verify that Axiom 6 application at N=I′ produces α/β=1; finite
part_b_equal_exponents.BOUNDARY: α = β for any two components is not falsified by their being individually non-1
part_b_equal_exponents.CONTEXT: Stage 3 applies Marginal Symmetry (A-MK1.6): setting ∂f/∂N = ∂f/∂I′ at N=I′ gives (α/β)×1^(β−α) = 1, hence α = β. Symmetry across all pairs gives α = β = γ = δ.

part_c_alpha_equals_one.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS a parsimony argument P SUCH THAT (P produces a canonical exponent α ≠ 1) AND (P is strictly stronger than the Scale Normalization Condition under face-value proportionality) AND (α ≠ 1 is consistent with Axioms A-MK1.1 through A-MK1.6)

WHERE.Scale_Normalization_Condition: modeling principle: scoring function is linear in each normalized component at face value; α = 1 selected because it is the only value under which component value x contributes x-proportionally to the score
WHERE.strictly_stronger: P produces a unique canonical value and relies on fewer additional assumptions than face-value proportionality

part_c_alpha_equals_one.EVALUATION: Check whether any proposed α ≠ 1 satisfies all six axioms; check whether proposed parsimony principle is more parsimonious than face-value linearity; assess whether it produces a unique canonical selection; finite
part_c_alpha_equals_one.BOUNDARY: Demonstrating α ≠ 1 is axiom-consistent does NOT falsify — the paper already concedes this (any α > 0 is consistent). Falsification requires a STRONGER parsimony argument, not merely an alternative one.
part_c_alpha_equals_one.CONTEXT: Part (c) is a canonical modeling choice. The paper's Correction 2 explicitly states: 'α = 1 is the canonical modeling choice under proportional normalization, not a uniquely forced mathematical conclusion.' This claim has weaker epistemic status than parts (a) and (b) and should be read accordingly.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Theorem 4.2 — Self-Referential Applicability
claim_registry.statement: Let S_MK denote the Meta-Kaizen process itself as a substrate. Then KVS is well-defined for improvement candidates targeting S_MK.

derivation_trace.operation: IR-4: Definition Expansion — substrate definition requires tuple (D, P, Ω, M)
derivation_trace.output: Verification target: does S_MK satisfy each component?

derivation_trace.operation: IR-2: Universal Instantiation — each component of S_MK tuple is instantiated from available definitions
derivation_trace.output: D_MK, P_MK, Ω_MK, M_MK each defined (see WHERE clause)

derivation_trace.operation: IR-1: Modus Ponens — if S_MK satisfies substrate definition, KVS is computable for its improvement candidates
derivation_trace.output: KVS is well-defined for candidates targeting S_MK □

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: O = {Ω_MK undefined: no metric exists for 'ranking fidelity of KVS'} → theorem falsified. Assessment: Ω_MK is defined as the historical log of scored candidates versus realized outcomes, which is a measurable variable once sufficient data accumulates — NOT FALSIFIED (Ω_MK is defined even if initially empty) → PASS

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: S_MK FAILS to satisfy the substrate definition D-MK1.1 — specifically, any of the four tuple components (D_MK, P_MK, Ω_MK, M_MK) is undefined or ill-formed

WHERE.D_MK: document set = KVS formula specification and rotation schedule
WHERE.P_MK: principal set = adopting organization's governance principals
WHERE.\u03a9_MK: outcome space = ranking fidelity of KVS against realized improvement quality
WHERE.M_MK: metric function = historical log of scored candidates versus outcomes
WHERE.well_defined: each of N, I′, C′, T is computable for an improvement candidate targeting S_MK under D-MK1.3 through D-MK1.6

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Check whether D_MK, P_MK, Ω_MK, M_MK are each definable; check whether N, I′, C′, T are computable for a candidate targeting S_MK; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: Theorem establishes well-definedness only, not convergence or optimality — convergence is addressed in Paper 2 (Governance Closure Theorem). A demonstration that KVS does not converge to an optimal formula does NOT falsify this theorem.
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: Self-referentiality is the mechanism that permits Meta-Kaizen to improve itself. The principal gate (human review required for all adoptions including meta-level ones) prevents runaway self-modification.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Theorem 4.3 — Threshold Optimality (Symmetric Case)
claim_registry.statement: When L_FP = L_FN (symmetric losses), π = 0.5 (uniform quality prior), and score distributions are symmetric around κ = 0.5 such that p_G(0.5) = p_P(0.5), the unique optimal adoption threshold is κ* = 0.50.

derivation_trace.operation: IR-4: Definition Expansion — adopt Bayesian decision framework (Duda, Hart & Stork, 2001); define R(κ), G, P, π
IR-5: Algebraic Manipulation — differentiate R(κ) with respect to κ
IR-5: Algebraic Manipulation — set dR/dκ = 0 → likelihood ratio condition p_G(κ*)/p_P(κ*) = L_FP(1−π)/(L_FN×π)
IR-3: Substitution of Equals — substitute symmetric conditions: L_FP = L_FN, π = 0.5, p_G(0.5) = p_P(0.5) → LR = 1
IR-1: Modus Ponens — LR = 1 → κ* = 0.50 □

claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: O = {under stated symmetric conditions, dR/dκ = 0 at κ = 0.45} → examine whether substitution error or distributional assumption violation → assessment requires checking each algebraic step in derivation

WHERE.n_invalid_steps_Bayes: integer · count of invalid steps in the Bayesian derivation in Section 4.7: valid iff output follows from inputs by IR-1 through IR-7
WHERE.stated_conditions: L_FP = L_FN AND π = 0.50 AND p_G(κ) = p_P(κ) at κ = 0.50
WHERE.kappa_star: scalar · the threshold minimizing expected loss R(κ) = L_FP × P(s≥κ|P) × (1−π) + L_FN × P(s<κ|G) × π

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Trace Bayesian derivation step-by-step; verify dR/dκ = 0 at κ = 0.50 under stated conditions; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: Asymmetric case (L_FP ≠ L_FN) uses approximation formula requiring distributional assumptions — asymmetric formula is NOT part of this predicate and is presented in the paper as an approximation, not a derivation
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: Symmetric special case is a complete derivation. Asymmetric formula requires distributional assumptions (uniform score distributions, uniform quality prior) stated explicitly in the paper. Practitioners using asymmetric thresholds must derive κ* from their specific loss ratio.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Simulation Result — KVS Recall under Q_mult_noisy
claim_registry.statement: KVS achieves 73.8% top-5% recall [90% CI: 71.2, 76.2] against Q_mult_noisy (multiplicative ground truth with log-normal noise σ=0.30) under the simulation specification in Section 5.2 with numpy seed=42.
claim_registry.epistemic_status_note: Simulation result — deterministic under stated seed. Not empirical outcome data. Establishes structural consistency of the scoring algorithm, not predictive validity. The 90% CI is the empirical interval across 1,000 independent runs (not a distributional assumption).
claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: O = {re-implementation with identical seed produces 65.0% recall} → outside [71.2, 76.2] → FALSIFIED — investigate whether distribution parameters or Q_mult_noisy formula differ from specification

WHERE.recall_KVS_Qmult: scalar · percent · mean KVS top-5% recall against Q_mult_noisy across 1,000 runs

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Re-implement simulation per Section 5.2 with numpy seed=42; compute KVS top-5% recall against Q_mult_noisy; average across 1,000 runs; check whether mean falls within [71.2, 76.2]; finite and reproducible
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: Recall = 71.2 or 76.2 → NOT FALSIFIED (CI bounds are inclusive). Result outside CI with numpy seed=42 indicates implementation error, not model falsification — this is a reproducibility claim.
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: This is a structural simulation result, not an empirical validation. The 73.8% figure is earned (not circular: Q_mult_noisy uses log-normal noise, not KVS.copy()). Comparison: ADD achieves 68.4% [66.0, 70.8] — non-overlapping CIs establish meaningful structural advantage. Random selector achieves 5% by construction.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Simulation Result — KVS Outperforms ADD under Q_threshold (all distributional variants)
claim_registry.statement: KVS maintains recall advantage over ADD under Q_threshold (Q=1 iff all factors > 0.5, else Q=0) across all six distributional parameter variants tested in Section 5.3. Baseline: KVS = 58.8% [56.1, 61.5] vs ADD = 51.2% [48.6, 54.1] — 7.5 pp advantage with non-overlapping confidence intervals.
claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: O = {Skewed-high-N variant: KVS=40.8% vs ADD=37.7%} → KVS > ADD → NOT FALSIFIED. O = {hypothetical: ADD=41.0% in this variant} → FALSIFIED

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS distributional_variant V IN {Baseline, Skewed-low-N, Skewed-high-N, Low-impact, High-impact, Wide-impact-spread} SUCH THAT (recall_KVS_Qthresh(V) ≤ recall_ADD_Qthresh(V))

WHERE.recall_KVS_Qthresh_V: scalar · percent · mean KVS top-5% recall against Q_threshold for variant V
WHERE.recall_ADD_Qthresh_V: scalar · percent · mean ADD top-5% recall against Q_threshold for variant V

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Re-implement simulation for each variant; compute KVS and ADD recall under Q_threshold; check whether KVS > ADD for every variant; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: KVS = ADD for any variant → FALSIFIED (claim requires strict advantage)
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: Q_threshold is the most practically informative outcome function: it formalizes that real improvements require minimum thresholds across ALL dimensions. KVS's structural advantage under Q_threshold is the primary simulation-based argument for the multiplicative form over additive alternatives.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Simulation Result — Timeliness Dominance Artifact
claim_registry.statement: Timeliness T correlates with KVS at ρ = 0.740 [90% CI: 0.730, 0.749] under baseline simulation specification — substantially higher than N (ρ=0.258), I′ (ρ=0.217), C′ (ρ=0.385). This is a distributional artifact explained by T=0 candidates (~17% of draws) collapsing KVS to zero.
claim_registry.sample_falsification_observation: O = {re-implementation produces ρ_T_KVS = 0.61} → outside [0.730, 0.749] → FALSIFIED — check whether h distribution specification (Uniform[0,72]) matches or differs

WHERE.rho_T_KVS: scalar · Spearman rank correlation between T and KVS across baseline simulation run
WHERE.pct_T_zero: scalar · percent · fraction of candidates receiving T=0 in baseline simulation (Uniform[0,72] h, H_max=60 → 12/72 = 16.7%)

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Re-run baseline simulation with numpy seed=42; compute Spearman ρ between T and KVS; count T=0 candidates; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: ρ_T = 0.730 → NOT FALSIFIED (CI lower bound is inclusive)
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: Operational remedy: filter T=0 candidates before scoring. Equal-weight property holds for candidates with T > 0. This artifact is a structural consequence of the distributional specification, not a model failure.

claim_registry.label: [TYPE:F]
claim_registry.name: Pre-Registered H₁ — Empirical Validation Path
claim_registry.statement: H₁: Among improvement candidates meeting KVS ≥ κ threshold, Spearman rank correlation between KVS score and realized outcome improvement (at 12 months post-adoption) is significantly greater than zero, stratified by substrate type. Minimum sample: n ≥ 30 per substrate per organization, across ≥ 10 organizations, minimum 300 observations per substrate.
claim_registry.placeholder_note: Empirical data does not yet exist. This claim unblocks when sufficient outcome data accumulates (estimated 3–4 years for high-frequency substrates). Reporting commitment: results will be reported regardless of direction. This placeholder does NOT block PHASE-READY — it is a pre-registered future empirical test.

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: (Spearman_rho_KVS_outcome ≤ 0) OR (p_value > 0.05) FOR any substrate type with n ≥ 300

WHERE.Spearman_rho_KVS_outcome: scalar · dimensionless · Spearman rank correlation between KVS score at time of adoption and realized outcome improvement measured at 12 months post-adoption
WHERE.n: integer · count of adopted improvements per substrate per organization, aggregated across ≥ 10 organizations
WHERE.p_value: scalar · from two-sided Spearman rank correlation test

falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: When n ≥ 300 per substrate: compute Spearman ρ between KVS scores (at proposal time) and realized outcomes (at 12-month review); test against zero; stratify by substrate type; finite
falsification_predicate.BOUNDARY: ρ = 0 or p = 0.05 → FALSIFIED (requires strictly greater than zero with significance)
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: If H₁ is not supported, the paper commits to recharacterizing KVS as a governance tool (transparent, auditable, consistent) rather than a scientific measurement instrument. This is explicitly stated in Section 5.4. Non-confirmation does not invalidate the axiomatic derivation — it bounds the empirical claim.

placeholder_register.description: Pre-registered empirical validation. H₁ requires n ≥ 300 adopted improvements per substrate type. Not yet achievable — high-frequency substrates estimated 3–4 years; financial governance may require a decade.
placeholder_register.target_session: OPEN — resolves when empirical data accumulates

dependency_edges.note: Eliminates additive form — the critical step
KVS formula is derived result of Theorem 4.1
Weak order is foundational to decision theory application
Q_threshold is structurally aligned with Axiom 5 — both require minimum thresholds across all dimensions
T=0 behavior is a direct consequence of T definition
H₁ tests whether the derived KVS predicts real outcomes — empirical test of the derivation's practical validity

channel_2_query_trace.description: Demonstration that an AI system can verify Claim F-MK1.1 (Boundedness) using this AI layer alone — zero prose references required. This trace resolves PH-DRP.2 (DRP-1 Section 7.4 live instance requirement).
channel_2_query_trace.query: Is claim F-MK1.1 (KVS Boundedness) verified for an improvement candidate with N=0.7, I′=0.6, C′=0.8, T=1.0?
channel_2_query_trace.step_3: Read FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS improvement candidate j SUCH THAT (KVS_j < 0) OR (KVS_j > 1)
channel_2_query_trace.step_4: Read WHERE: KVS_j = N_j × I′_j × C′_j × T_j per D-MK1.2
channel_2_query_trace.step_6: Check: 0.336 ∈ [0,1] — TRUE. FALSIFIED_IF condition not met.
channel_2_query_trace.step_7: Read derivation_trace: Step 1 (IR-4) + Step 2 (IR-5) — both valid for this candidate
channel_2_query_trace.result: F-MK1.1 NOT FALSIFIED for this candidate. Verification complete.

process_graph_excerpt.description: Minimal process graph entry for MK-P1 — for insertion into process-graph.json

ir_gap_register.description: Scale Normalization Condition (selection of α=1) uses a modeling principle (parsimony / face-value proportionality) that cannot be classified under IR-1 through IR-7.
ir_gap_register.proposed_resolution: Propose IR-8: Parsimony / Modeling Principle Selection — a step that selects a canonical value from a continuous family of axiom-consistent options based on a named parsimony principle. Valid iff: (a) the family of consistent options is established; (b) the parsimony principle is named and cited; (c) the selection is documented as a canonical choice, not a necessary derivation.
ir_gap_register.note: Paper already discloses this limitation in Correction 2. The gap is transparent. The AI layer flags it for governance completeness.
ir_gap_register.resolved_note: IR-8 added to inference rule inventory by CBT Amendment A-S43-2 (Session 43).
